questions.question: In Swift, 'Structs' are value types and 'Classes' are reference types.
questions.explanation: This is a fundamental difference: Structs are copied when passed around, while Classes share the same instance.

questions.question: The 'guard' statement is used for early exits from a function.
questions.explanation: Guard helps keep code clean by handling failure cases up front and requiring a return or break.

questions.question: You use the 'throws' keyword to indicate a function can return an error.
questions.explanation: Throwing functions must be called using 'try' within a do-catch block.

questions.question: A 'Protocol' defines a blueprint of methods or properties without implementing them.
questions.explanation: Types then 'conform' to the protocol by providing the actual implementation.

questions.question: Swift requires a semicolon ';' at the end of every line.
questions.explanation: Semicolons are optional and rarely used in Swift, except to separate multiple statements on a single line.

questions.question: The 'self' keyword refers to the current instance of a type.
questions.explanation: It is used within methods to refer to properties or other methods of the same instance.
